```
        match args.[0] with
        | "Csv" ->
            Csv { Sample = args.[1]
                  Separators = args.[2]
                  InferRows = Int32.MaxValue
                  Schema = args.[3].Replace(';', ',')
                  HasHeaders = args.[4] |> bool.Parse
                  IgnoreErrors = false
                  SkipRows = 0
                  AssumeMissingValues = args.[5] |> bool.Parse
                  PreferOptionals = args.[6] |> bool.Parse
                  Quote = '"'
                  MissingValues = args.[7]
                  Culture = args.[8]
                  Encoding = args.[9]
                  CacheRows = false
                  ResolutionFolder = ""
                  EmbeddedResource = ""
                  PreferDateOnly = false }

```

Can you just embed symbols instead of the separate package? That's much less hassle for consumers. Separate packages make Sourcelink hard to use.

From https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/nuget/create-packages/symbol-packages-snupkg

> The legacy format .symbols.nupkg is still supported but only for compatibility reasons like native packages (see [Legacy Symbol Packages](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/nuget/create-packages/symbol-packages)). NuGet.org's symbol server only accepts the new symbol package format - .snupkg.

So I can't work out why having this in fails the package build when there are two packages.

Without it we get `FSharp.Data.Core.symbols.nupkg` and `FSharp.Data.symbols.nupkg` correctly built but the file name extensions are different

The problem is, even if we emit zeros for the time component, the resulting xml would be invalid.

Maybe we could use the "yyyy-MM-dd" format when the time component is zero and use the "O" format otherwise.

We only want to ignore the time component when xs:date was specified on a XSD, but when a DateTime was inferred from a sample file, there might be a time component, and this code is common to both. I think it would be safer to always output even if it means emitting an 00:00:00 time all the time

If we make `System.DateTime` correspond to `xs:date` (and `System.DateTimeOffset` correspond to `xs:dateTime`) when formatting a `xs:date` we have to avoid the time component in order to produce valid xml according to the XML Schema specification. On the other hand, when formatting a `xs:dateTime` it's ok to use the `"O"` format that is compliant with the W3C specs for `xs:dateTime`
Why this change?
Sorry, I'd like to stay in net45 as the min supported version for a while longer, can you revert that part of the change?
I also prefer 4
we can also have paket manage the paket-file includes, mapping the particular file into this directory at the specified path (in this case ./ProvidedTypes.fs)
we can use the `framework: xxxx` syntax to use particular versions of FSharp.Core (or any other package) for a particular framework. Here we use 4.3.4 for netstandard2.0, and 4.0.0.1 for net462.
because we switched back to paket we can have it manage our file includes as well. see the `paket.references` for details.
we can manage our clitool dependencies in paket like this now.

We only have three target frameworks and so if we list them here lockfiles and such get a bit more sane.
In addition, I bumped us up to net462 since I got a lot of errors around referencing System.Object and System.Runtime on net45. This i think is the most contentious change, and I'd be glad to discuss more.

These files are largely mechanical. The next step here would be to move from the monolithic FAKE dependency to a paket build group with just the few modules we use (which mostly map 1-to-1 with the `open Fake.xxx` statements) and move to the Fake 5 netcore runner.

There were a few places in the project files where we needed a common mapping from build-time target frameworks to what the TPSDK-conventions are.  So I declared them here, and this file is picked up and included by every project file underneath this directory.
here's where we'd decide on 2 vs 4. I prefer 4, but I'll go with whatever we settle on.

Since this is an erasing type provider, using this define makes the design-time DLL smaller.  

But yes, we could now optionally make this a generative type provider.  That would be separate thing and I think would have to be revealed as a separately named TP, since whether a TP is generative or not is determined before the static parameters are applied:
```fsharp
type MyXml = XmlProviderGenerative<"foo.xml">
```
Besides I believe it would be easy (it may stilll uncover bugs in generative TP support, though that's pretty solid - Swagger has ironed out the issues).

.NET Standard 2.0 is a big and large common library subset supported supported by .NET Framework 4.6+, Mono (including Xamarin Android/iOS) and .NET Core. It has a normal reflection model and has pretty much everything you actually need to build most libraries, and looks and feels like normal .NET 4.x programming for nearly all purposes.  All of FSharp.Data compiles without any `#if` when targeting .NET Standard 2.0.  It's basically what PCLs should always have been.

.NET Core App 2.0 (netcoreapp2.0) is the name of the programming model for a typical .NET Core application.  The programming model is basically the same as .NET Standard 2.0 but in theory you could have access to extra stuff that is not supported by .NET Standard (e.g. cross-platform ways of accessing .NET Core specifics about the .NET Core GC or something).

Ok, I figured out the stream closure stuff. `use` statements now only ever happen when a function expects to consume the stream.  this really only happens in a few places: 

* `toHttpResponse` for the string/binary response body types
* `asyncRead`, where we read a stream into memory locally
* `decompressGZip` and `decompressDeflate`, where we consume a stream and deflate it into a new memorystream

all of these are terminal in some form or another, consuming the stream and replacing it with some other representation.

now the `toHttpResponse` used by the stream variant of the response body allows `asyncRead` to close the originating stream as part of its work, so using `asyncRead` is a bit safer now.

I don't think we can, actually.  The FX_NO_LOCAL_FILESYSTEM define is set in `src/DefineConstants.targets` if the target profile is 47, 7, or 259. As long as we support PCLs here I think we should maintain this code.

I m sorry to bother you, but according to [@forki comment](https://github.com/fsharp/FSharp.Data/pull/1044#issuecomment-293246058), this environment variable is checked only on Windows. There's no sense in setting it on Linux build.

To make it more clear:
Executing this in 10 threads within 10secs will take gigabyte of memory even when cache is ConcurrentDictionary:

``` fsharp
match cache.TryRetrieve typeName with 
| Some x -> x 
| None -> 
   let x = long_10sec_call_taking_100MB_of_temp_memory()
   cache <- x
   x
```

Because cache is accessed twice in the code and ConcurrentDictionary doesn't have lock over multiple calls.
This is exactly the same functionality, but taking just 100MB even when done in multiple threads:

``` fsharp
cache.GetOrAdd(typeName, fun typeName -> 
   long_10sec_call_taking_100MB_of_temp_memory())
```

For that reason I think current ICache<> is a bad idea.
Should it be? Maybe that is one problem of memory consumption that there is similar dictionaries in cache when executed every time separately in nested data structures.
What if Visual Studio has been closed? Can it be null (cleaned by VS) and still have the async task in the memory?

Because we are using non-UI action we don't have to work in UI thread. We can use TPL.
So when the async operation is over, we can use any available thread to clean the dictionary, instead of limiting our choice to wait the only-UI-thread.

For me, the NuGet-version of XMLProvider was unusable to work with (yes it works in general, but it made Visual Studio so slow that I couldn't do my task, VS consumed over 2GB of memory in 10mins work, and changing the XML-file caused VS to crash every time). I can work with my current version so I'm happy with it and I just wanted to share the modifications. 

I'm not currently having time to debug and fix the actual cause of problems but I think it has somethig to do with leaking caches and the fact that TypeProviders are executed in multiple threads in VS and too much code is run just when you call a single constructed-type constructor inside other constructed-type constructors in the code corresponding the XML DOM-tree.

So if you'll do a new release that is performing better, I'll change to that but before it I will continue using the version in my fork.

What is the pont of using ConcurrentDictionary at the first place if you are not taking advantage of using GetOrAdd or AddOrUpdate methods which are guaranteeing the concistency by using the internal locks surrounding the lambda functions? I think that the abstraction level of the ICache interface is wrong.

The point of AddOrUpdate is to make it clear that on update-case the old value is being overridden by purpose. Of course it would be better to throw the ICache away and just use GetOrAdd.
If res is null, the type guard would never succeed
Unlike the watchers and the global caches, this dictionary is not ever accessed from multiple threads, so we don't need the concurrentDictionary here, this is a locally scoped item
Can you please pu the watchers.add and watchers.Remove inside a lock on watchers in the FX_NO_CONCURRENT branch?
watcher can never be null, please remove the null check
This async block was missing and will fix a memory leek. Can you do do the same on the other implementation above that doesn't use ConcurrentDictionary?
The AddOrUpdate doesn't give us nothing here, as you are ignoring the old value when calculating the new one
You don't need the separate lock here as dict is not accessible externally
We should lazy for values which are initialized but might not be needed on all the forward the code paths, but I don't think it makes sense to use lazy on the output of functions
Can you explain this change?
Given that the new value being added is not dependent on the old value, there is no gain in using the more complex AddOrUpdate overload
You are applying the lazy to the output of the function. There's only going to ever be one thread accessing that. Please revert

The issue with locking directly an object, is that if that object is accessible outside some external code may lock it as well, causing deadlocks. That's not the case here, as disposeActions is a local value binding, so please revert

That's right it shouldn't be. I was getting ObjectDisposedExceptions while I had debugger attached to FSharp.Data and breaking to every exception. So I assumed that some async method like this does try to handle object that is already disposed. But clearly not this one as the dictionary is static.
Lazy also should guarantee that there are no other thread at the same time running the same code with the same parameters. https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/dd997286(v=vs.110).aspx
Book "CLR via C#" has described that there is a bug in .NET framework that if the locking object is used directly it may cause deadlock and for that reason you should use separate locking object.

Try running this in FSI:

``` fsharp
let isCharacterEscapable (c:char) =
    (* CSS 2.1: Any character (except a hexadecimal digit, linefeed,
       carriage return, or form feed) can be escaped with a backslash to
       remove its special meaning *)
    let isHexadecimalDigit = Char.IsDigit(c) || (Char.ToLower(c) >= 'a' && Char.ToLower(c) <= 'f')
    (isHexadecimalDigit || c = '\n' || c = '\f' || c = '\r')
    |> not

let isEscapable = isCharacterEscapable '\''
```
but `isCharacterEscapable` will return false for `'`, right?

Doesn't the new code supersede it? It would escape the `'` character that followed `\` either in quotes or not in quotes all the same, as the code would escape any character `\x` where `x` is not a hexadecimal digit nor `LF`, `RF`, `FF`
Don't you need to also keep this rule?
= or .Equals is equivalent in this case

The "default" default is 100s, but you're right in that we can override it with `Timeout.Infinite` at the time the request is created. For some reason I thought these functions should work with any provided `HttpWebRequest`, but it doesn't seem to be the case and we're creating it internally.

I'll take a look at doing these changes at some point next week.

I see. I do like the idea of having optional `timeout` parameter - that sounds like something that would be quite useful for many people anyway!

That said, if the value is the default, then it probably should be `Timeout.Infinite`, so we could just check for that and use async only when it is not infinite - that should work too.

I'm happy with either of the options.

I don't think detecting `req.Timeout` is an option here - it's a has a meaningful default value when not set, so you can't really differentiate based on that. And the thing with "normal synchronous way" here is that it's really the "async way" being run synchronously. This is different to plain old `HttpWebRequest.GetResponse` in that it doesn't take timeout into account, default or not. 

What I can do instead is scrap the idea of using `HttpWebRequest.Timeout` and instead have an optional timeout argument to the `Http` static members at the end of the file. I suppose making the timeout explicit would help more people than it would confuse.

Also, would it be possible to somehow detect whether `req.Timeout` has been set and do the custom logic only when it is set? (And call the operation in a normal synchronous way otherwise.) I guess it could have some small overhead associated - not a big deal if this is hard to do though.
Yeah, pushed another commit.

For parsing speed, I tend to use a struct like

```
[<Struct>]
type State(source: string, pos: string) =
    member __.Source = source
    member __.Pos = pos
```
